CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): Overall the manuscript is organized in a standard, logical structure (Introduction → Related Work → Method → Training → Experiments → Conclusion) and states the main contribution (Collaborative Deep Embedding with dual networks) early in the Introduction. The notation is mostly consistent and key equations (1)–(5) are defined, but presentation suffers from distracting copy/paste artifacts, duplicated sentences and citation strings (many repeated lines toward the end), small typos (e.g. “for for”, “WML” vs. WMF), and missing referenced figures/tables in the provided text (e.g. Figure 1 and Tables 1–3 are cited but not shown). These issues make some sections harder to follow and give the paper a need for careful proofreading and inclusion/placement of the referenced figures and tables. | B(4): Overall the paper is well-organized and follows a clear logical flow (Introduction → Related Work → Method → Training → Experiments → Conclusion), and the main contributions (Collaborative Deep Embedding, dual encoding networks, multi-level/branching design and better generalization) are stated early and repeatedly. Notation is mostly consistent and key equations (WMF, user encoding, joint objective) are clearly introduced, and experimental setup and metrics are well described. Presentation issues are minor but noticeable: several typos/oddities (e.g. “WML” vs WMF, “for for”), a number of duplicated/repeated sentences near the end, and references to figures/tables without the actual visuals in the provided text, which hurt readability and final polish.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the manuscript is organized in a standard, logical structure (Introduction → Related Work → Method → Training → Experiments → Conclusion) and states the main contribution (Collaborative Deep Embedding with dual networks) early in the Introduction. The notation is mostly consistent and key equations (1)–(5) are defined, but presentation suffers from distracting copy/paste artifacts, duplicated sentences and citation strings (many repeated lines toward the end), small typos (e.g. “for for”, “WML” vs. WMF), and missing referenced figures/tables in the provided text (e.g. Figure 1 and Tables 1–3 are cited but not shown). These issues make some sections harder to follow and give the paper a need for careful proofreading and inclusion/placement of the referenced figures and tables.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well-organized and follows a clear logical flow (Introduction → Related Work → Method → Training → Experiments → Conclusion), and the main contributions (Collaborative Deep Embedding, dual encoding networks, multi-level/branching design and better generalization) are stated early and repeatedly. Notation is mostly consistent and key equations (WMF, user encoding, joint objective) are clearly introduced, and experimental setup and metrics are well described. Presentation issues are minor but noticeable: several typos/oddities (e.g. “WML” vs WMF, “for for”), a number of duplicated/repeated sentences near the end, and references to figures/tables without the actual visuals in the provided text, which hurt readability and final polish.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes a notable extension beyond prior work by jointly learning paired deep encoders for both items and users (ui = g(ri;Wu) and f(x;Wv)) rather than only encoding items as in CDL, directly addressing cold-start/generalization (see Sec.3 and comparison with CDL). The multi-level branching architecture and the dual mini-batch training procedure are non-trivial technical contributions that go beyond straightforward combinations of existing techniques (Sec.3.2, Sec.4). Overall this is a clear, differentiated advance over WMF/CDL but not a radical departure from existing deep-CF hybrids, so its novelty is notable rather than groundbreaking. | B(4): The paper proposes a clearly new configuration: jointly learned dual encoding networks for items and users (user encoding via g(r;W_u) in Eq.3 and joint objective Eq.4), which directly contrasts with prior work like CDL that only deep-encodes items. It also introduces non-trivial architectural and training ideas (multi-level aligned embeddings with branching in Sec.3.2 and the dual mini-batch training in Sec.4) that go beyond simple application of existing autoencoder/CF components. While the approach composes known building blocks (SDAE/MLP, inner-product matching), the combination and the explicit deep user-encoder provide notable novelty rather than a purely incremental tweak.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a notable extension beyond prior work by jointly learning paired deep encoders for both items and users (ui = g(ri;Wu) and f(x;Wv)) rather than only encoding items as in CDL, directly addressing cold-start/generalization (see Sec.3 and comparison with CDL). The multi-level branching architecture and the dual mini-batch training procedure are non-trivial technical contributions that go beyond straightforward combinations of existing techniques (Sec.3.2, Sec.4). Overall this is a clear, differentiated advance over WMF/CDL but not a radical departure from existing deep-CF hybrids, so its novelty is notable rather than groundbreaking.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a clearly new configuration: jointly learned dual encoding networks for items and users (user encoding via g(r;W_u) in Eq.3 and joint objective Eq.4), which directly contrasts with prior work like CDL that only deep-encodes items. It also introduces non-trivial architectural and training ideas (multi-level aligned embeddings with branching in Sec.3.2 and the dual mini-batch training in Sec.4) that go beyond simple application of existing autoencoder/CF components. While the approach composes known building blocks (SDAE/MLP, inner-product matching), the combination and the explicit deep user-encoder provide notable novelty rather than a purely incremental tweak.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims (dual deep encoders, multi-level branching, and improved generalization) are clearly stated and supported by empirical results on three real-world datasets (Sec.5) showing consistent recall gains over WMF and CDL. The key mathematical point (user representation as a transform of history and replacement by a deep encoder, Eq.2–3) is correct and the optimization objective (Eq.4) is coherent. Experimental methodology is reasonable (standard datasets, tuned baselines, ablations), but the paper omits statistical significance testing, some hyperparameter/training-details (e.g. batch sizes, epochs) and compares to a limited set of modern baselines; also the claim that the objective “directly targets recommendation accuracy” is somewhat overstated since squared loss is not perfectly aligned with ranking/recall. Overall the evidence supports the conclusions modulo these minor methodological gaps. | B(4): The paper’s main claims (dual deep encoders, multi-level branching, and improved in-/out-of-matrix recommendation) are clearly stated and supported by experiments on CiteULike, MovieLens+Posters and Ciao (e.g. large Recall@M gains and a 47.6% relative improvement on out-matrix CiteULike). The mathematical formulation and derivations (WMF review, closed-form user solution in Eq.2, and the proposed objective Eq.4) are straightforward and correct. Experimental methodology is generally sound (reasonable baselines WMF/CDL, validation-based hyperparameter search, in-/out-matrix splits), but lacks statistical significance reporting, broader baseline coverage, and clarity on some training/weighting choices (e.g. choices of cij, handling of cold-start users). Overall the results justify the conclusions, with only minor issues in experimental rigor and some untested assumptions about new-user encoding.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims (dual deep encoders, multi-level branching, and improved generalization) are clearly stated and supported by empirical results on three real-world datasets (Sec.5) showing consistent recall gains over WMF and CDL. The key mathematical point (user representation as a transform of history and replacement by a deep encoder, Eq.2–3) is correct and the optimization objective (Eq.4) is coherent. Experimental methodology is reasonable (standard datasets, tuned baselines, ablations), but the paper omits statistical significance testing, some hyperparameter/training-details (e.g. batch sizes, epochs) and compares to a limited set of modern baselines; also the claim that the objective “directly targets recommendation accuracy” is somewhat overstated since squared loss is not perfectly aligned with ranking/recall. Overall the evidence supports the conclusions modulo these minor methodological gaps.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims (dual deep encoders, multi-level branching, and improved in-/out-of-matrix recommendation) are clearly stated and supported by experiments on CiteULike, MovieLens+Posters and Ciao (e.g. large Recall@M gains and a 47.6% relative improvement on out-matrix CiteULike). The mathematical formulation and derivations (WMF review, closed-form user solution in Eq.2, and the proposed objective Eq.4) are straightforward and correct. Experimental methodology is generally sound (reasonable baselines WMF/CDL, validation-based hyperparameter search, in-/out-matrix splits), but lacks statistical significance reporting, broader baseline coverage, and clarity on some training/weighting choices (e.g. choices of cij, handling of cold-start users). Overall the results justify the conclusions, with only minor issues in experimental rigor and some untested assumptions about new-user encoding.
